name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِن نَّتَّبِعِ ٱلْهُدَىٰ مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنَآ ۚ أَوَلَمْ نُمَكِّن لَّهُمْ حَرَمًا ءَامِنًا يُجْبَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ ثَمَرَٰتُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ رِّزْقًا مِّن لَّدُنَّا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٥٧)
```

```english
They say, ‘If we follow the guidance with you, we will be forced out of our territory.’ Did We not establish a secure sanctuary for them where fruits of all kinds are brought as a provision from Us? But most of them do not know. (57)
```

```arabic
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍۭ بَطِرَتْ مَعِيشَتَهَا ۖ فَتِلْكَ مَسَـٰكِنُهُمْ لَمْ تُسْكَن مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا ۖ وَكُنَّا نَحْنُ ٱلْوَٰرِثِينَ (٥٨)
```

```english
How many a town We have destroyed that exulted in its lifestyle! There lie their dwellings, uninhabited after them except by a few, and We were the [sole] inheritors. (58)
```

```arabic
وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ مُهْلِكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ يَبْعَثَ فِىٓ أُمِّهَا رَسُولًا يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا ۚ وَمَا كُنَّا مُهْلِكِى ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ إِلَّا وَأَهْلُهَا ظَـٰلِمُونَ (٥٩)
```

```english
Your Lord would not destroy the towns until He had raised an apostle in their mother city to recite to them Our signs. We would never destroy the towns except when their people were wrongdoers. (59)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أُوتِيتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَمَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتُهَا ۚ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٦٠)
```

```english
Whatever things you have been given are only the wares of the life of this world and its glitter, and what is with Allah is better and more lasting. Will you not exercise your reason? (60)
```

```arabic
أَفَمَن وَعَدْنَـٰهُ وَعْدًا حَسَنًا فَهُوَ لَـٰقِيهِ كَمَن مَّتَّعْنَـٰهُ مَتَـٰعَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ثُمَّ هُوَ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْضَرِينَ (٦١)
```

```english
Is he to whom We have given a good promise, which he will receive, like him whom We have provided the wares of the life of this world, but who will be arraigned on the Day of Resurrection? (61)
```

```arabic
وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ (٦٢)
```

```english
The day He will call out to them and ask, ‘Where are My ‘partners’ that you used to claim?’ (62)
```

```arabic
قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ رَبَّنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أَغْوَيْنَآ أَغْوَيْنَـٰهُمْ كَمَا غَوَيْنَا ۖ تَبَرَّأْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ۖ مَا كَانُوٓا۟ إِيَّانَا يَعْبُدُونَ (٦٣)
```

```english
Those against whom the word had become due will say, ‘Our Lord! These are the ones whom we have perverted. We perverted them as we were perverse ourselves. We repudiate them before You: it was not us that they worshipped.’ (63)
```

```arabic
وَقِيلَ ٱدْعُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَكُمْ فَدَعَوْهُمْ فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَهُمْ وَرَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۚ لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَهْتَدُونَ (٦٤)
```

```english
It will be said, ‘Invoke your partners!’ So they will invoke them, but they will not respond to them, and they will sight the punishment, wishing they had followed guidance. (64)
```

```arabic
وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ مَاذَآ أَجَبْتُمُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٦٥)
```

```english
The day He will call out to them and say, ‘What response did you give to the apostles?’ (65)
```

```arabic
فَعَمِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَنۢبَآءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَهُمْ لَا يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (٦٦)
```

```english
That day all information will be withheld from them, so they will not question one another. (66)
```

```arabic
فَأَمَّا مَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَعَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْلِحِينَ (٦٧)
```

```english
As for him who repents, has faith and acts righteously, maybe he will be among the felicitous. (67)
```

```arabic
وَرَبُّكَ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ وَيَخْتَارُ ۗ مَا كَانَ لَهُمُ ٱلْخِيَرَةُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٦٨)
```

```english
Your Lord creates whatever He wishes and chooses: they have no choice. Immaculate is Allah and exalted above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (68)
```

```arabic
وَرَبُّكَ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ (٦٩)
```

```english
Your Lord knows whatever their breasts conceal, and whatever they disclose. (69)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ فِى ٱلْأُولَىٰ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَلَهُ ٱلْحُكْمُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٧٠)
```

```english
He is Allah, there is no god except Him. All praise belongs to Him in this world and the Hereafter. All judgement belongs to Him, and to Him you will be brought back. (70)
```

```arabic
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ سَرْمَدًا إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَنْ إِلَـٰهٌ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُم بِضِيَآءٍ ۖ أَفَلَا تَسْمَعُونَ (٧١)
```

```english
Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah were to make the night perpetual for you until the Day of Resurrection, what god other than Allah can bring you light? Will you not listen?’ (71)
```

```arabic
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلنَّهَارَ سَرْمَدًا إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَنْ إِلَـٰهٌ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ يَأْتِيكُم بِلَيْلٍ تَسْكُنُونَ فِيهِ ۖ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٧٢)
```

```english
Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah were to make the day perpetual for you until the Day of Resurrection, what god other than Allah can bring you night wherein you can rest? Will you not perceive?’ (72)
```

```arabic
وَمِن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٧٣)
```

```english
He has made for you the night and the day out of His mercy, so that you may rest therein and that you may seek His bounty and so that you may give thanks. (73)
```

```arabic
وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ (٧٤)
```

```english
The day He will call out to them and say, ‘Where are My ‘partners’ that you used to claim?’ (74)
```

```arabic
وَنَزَعْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيدًا فَقُلْنَا هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ فَعَلِمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلْحَقَّ لِلَّهِ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٧٥)
```

```english
We shall draw from every nation a witness and say, ‘Produce your evidence.’ Then they will know that all reality belongs to Allah and what they used to fabricate will forsake them. (75)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses mention another excuse offered by the polytheists of Mecca for not believing in the Book of Allah, following the mention of their previous excuse: **لَوْ لا أُوتِيَ مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ مُوسى** (Why was he not given the like of what Moses was given?) and its refutation. This new excuse is their saying: "If we follow the guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — that your Book has brought, which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — the polytheists of the Arabs will snatch us away from our land through killing, captivity, plundering, and the stripping away of security and peace."

Allah, the Exalted, refuted this by stating that He has established for them a secure sanctuary (حرماً آمناً) which the Arabs respect, and to which the fruits of all things are brought; thus, there is no cause for their fear of being snatched away.

Furthermore, their enjoyment of wealth and children and their reckless exultation (بطر) in their livelihood does not guarantee them safety from destruction, such that they should prefer it over following the guidance. How many a town that exulted recklessly in its livelihood has Allah destroyed and uprooted, becoming its inheritor? Those are their dwellings, which have not been inhabited after them except a little.

Moreover, that which they prefer over following the guidance is merely the enjoyment (متاع) of the fleeting life of this world (الحياة الدنيا), and no rational person would choose it over the eternal life of the Hereafter (الحياة الآخرة) which is with Allah, the Glorified.

Additionally, creation (الخلق) and command (الأمر) belong to Allah; if He chooses something and commands it, no one has the right to oppose Him in favor of what their soul desires or what their nature inclines toward. Then, the Exalted cites the story of Qarun and how He caused the earth to swallow him and his dwelling.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَقالُوا إِنْ نَتَّبِعِ الْهُدى مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا** — **They say, ‘If we follow the guidance with you, we will be snatched away from our land.’** until the end of the verse. **Snatching away** (التخطف) means to seize something quickly. It is also said that *al-khatf* and *al-takhattuf* mean to despoil from every side. Their being "snatched away from their land" is a metaphor (استعارة) intended to mean killing, captivity, and the plundering of wealth, as if they and all that pertains to them of family and property are taken away, leaving their land empty of them. The "land" refers to the land of Mecca and the Sanctuary (الحرم), as evidenced by His subsequent saying: **أَوَلَمْ نُمَكِّنْ لَهُمْ حَرَماً آمِناً** (Have We not established for them a secure sanctuary?). The speakers were some of the polytheists of Mecca.

The sentence is presented as an excuse for not believing, claiming that if they believe, the Arabs will snatch them from their land—the land of Mecca—because the Arabs are polytheists who would not be pleased with their faith and their rejection of idols. This is a case of presenting an obstacle (المانع); it contains an admission of the truth of the core call (الدعوة) and that the Book is true in what it contains, but the danger of being snatched away is an obstacle to accepting it and believing in it. This is why they expressed it by saying: **إِنْ نَتَّبِعِ الْهُدى مَعَكَ** (If we follow the guidance with you) and did not say: "If we follow your Book" or "your religion" or something similar.

Regarding His saying: **أَوَلَمْ نُمَكِّنْ لَهُمْ حَرَماً آمِناً** — **Have We not established for them a secure sanctuary?** It is said that **establishing** (التمكين) here implies the meaning of "making" (الجعل); thus, the meaning is: "Have We not made for them a secure sanctuary, establishing them therein?" It is also said that "sanctuary" (حرماً) is an adverbial noun of place (ظرفية), meaning: "Have We not established for them *in* a sanctuary?" And **secure** (آمِناً) is an adjective for "sanctuary," meaning a sanctuary characterized by safety. Attributing safety to the sanctuary—while it is the people within it who are safe—is a case of metaphorical attribution (المجاز في النسبة). The sentence is coordinated with a suppressed clause, the estimation being: "Have We not protected them and made for them a secure sanctuary, establishing them therein?"

This is the first response from Allah, the Exalted, to their saying: **إِنْ نَتَّبِعِ الْهُدى مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا** (If we follow the guidance with you, we will be snatched away from our land). Its gist is: We have established them in a land that We made a sanctuary characterized by safety, which the Arabs respect; therefore, there is no reason for their fear of being snatched from it if they believe.

His saying: **يُجْبى إِلَيْهِ ثَمَراتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — **to which are brought the fruits of all things.** **Bringing** (الجباية) means gathering. The word "all" (كل) is for multiplicity (التكثير), not for absolute universality (العموم), as absolute universality is certainly not intended. The meaning is: the fruits of many things are gathered to the Sanctuary. This sentence is an adjective for "sanctuary," mentioned to dispel any illusion that they might be harmed if they believe due to the cessation of provisions (الميرة).

His saying: **رِزْقاً مِنْ لَدُنَّا** — **as a provision from Us** — is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) or a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for "fruits." His saying: **وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** — **but most of them do not know** — is a rectification (استدراك) regarding all that preceded. That is: We are the ones who preserved them in safety and provided them with all fruits, but most of them are ignorant of this, imagining that what protects them from being snatched by the Arabs is their polytheism and their worship of idols.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ بَطِرَتْ مَعِيشَتَها** — **How many a town We have destroyed that exulted recklessly in its heritage!** until the end of the verse. **Reckless exultation** (البطر) is transgression (الطغيان) upon receiving a blessing. **Its heritage** (مَعِيشَتَها) is in the accusative case due to the removal of the preposition (بنزع الخافض), meaning: "How many a town transgressed *in* its livelihood."

His saying: **فَتِلْكَ مَساكِنُهُمْ لَمْ تُسْكَنْ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً** — **There are their dwellings, uninhabited after them except a little.** That is: their ruined dwellings, collapsed upon their trellises, are visible before your eyes, remaining in their state of ruin, neither inhabited nor dwelt in after their destruction except for a small portion of them.

From this, it appears more appropriate that **except a little** (إِلَّا قَلِيلاً) is an exception from **their dwellings** (مَساكِنُهُمْ), not from His saying **after them** (مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ) — which would mean they were not inhabited after them except for a short time — because they are only inhabited by passers-by for a day or part of a day during travels.

His saying: **وَكُنَّا نَحْنُ الْوارِثِينَ** — **and We were the inheritors.** Since they possessed them and then left them, and no one succeeded them except Us, We inherited their dwellings. In this sentence, there is a subtle point (عناية لطيفة): for He, the Exalted, is the true and absolute owner of everything. He was the owner of their dwellings and gave them possession by granting them power over them, then He stripped them from their hands by destroying them, and they remained after them with no owner but Him. Thus, He called Himself the "Inheritor" (وارثاً) with the consideration that He is the One who remains after them, and He is the owner of what was in their hands; it is as if their nominal ownership (الملك الاعتباري) transferred to Him, though in reality there is no transfer, but rather His true ownership (الملك الحقيقي) became manifest through the disappearance of their nominal ownership.

The verse is a second response from Allah, the Exalted, to their saying: **إِنْ نَتَّبِعِ الْهُدى مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا**. Its gist is that the mere fact that the Arabs do not snatch you from your land does not guarantee your survival, nor does it preserve your land and your enjoyment therein as you wish. How many a town, reaching the heights of luxury and characterized by insolence and reckless exultation, have We destroyed its people, leaving their dwellings empty and uninhabited, with no inheritor but Allah.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَما كانَ رَبُّكَ مُهْلِكَ الْقُرى حَتَّى يَبْعَثَ فِي أُمِّها رَسُولاً** — **Your Lord would not destroy the towns until He had raised a messenger in their mother-city.** The "mother of towns" (أم القرى) is their origin and the largest among them to which they refer. The verse explains the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — *the established way of Allah* — regarding the destruction of towns by uprooting (الاستئصال): that the punishment of uprooting does not occur from Allah, the Exalted, except after the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة) against them by sending a messenger who recites to them the signs (آيات) of Allah, and only after the punished ones have been wrongdoers (ظالمين) by disbelieving in the signs of Allah and denying His messenger.

Following the previous verse with this one, which explains His sunnah in destroying towns, serves as a warning to the polytheists of Mecca, implying that if they persist in their disbelief, they are liable to the descent of punishment, because Allah has already raised in their mother-city—which is Mecca—a messenger reciting His signs to them, while they are nonetheless wrongdoers by denying their messenger.

From this, the subtle point (النكتة) in the shift (الالتفات) from the first-person plural to the third-person singular in His saying **وَما كانَ رَبُّكَ مُهْلِكَ الْقُرى** (Your Lord would not destroy the towns) becomes clear. The allusion to the fulfillment of the conditions for punishment among them if they deny the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) serves to strengthen him and emphasize his proof. As for the return afterward to the first-person plural in His saying **وَما كُنَّا مُهْلِكِي الْقُرى** (and We would not destroy the towns), it is a return to the previous style after the purpose was achieved.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَما أُوتِيتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَمَتاعُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** — **Whatever things you have been given are the wares of the life of this world** etc. **Giving** (الإيتاء) means bestowing. **Of anything** (مِنْ شَيْءٍ) explains "whatever" to indicate universality (العموم), meaning: everything you have been given. **Enjoyment** (المتاع) is that which is enjoyed, and **adornment** (الزينة) is that which is added to a thing to grant it beauty and grace. The "life of this world" (الحياة الدنيا) is the temporary, interrupted life that is the nearer of the two lives to us, contrasted with the life of the Hereafter (الحياة الآخرة) which is eternal and everlasting. What is "with Allah" refers to the felicitous life of the Hereafter in the proximity of Allah; hence it is described as "better and more lasting."

The meaning is: all the worldly blessings that Allah has given you are merely enjoyment and adornment with which this worldly life—the nearer of the two lives—is beautified, and it is perishing and fleeting. What is with Allah of His reward in the Abode of the Hereafter, resulting from following the guidance and believing in the signs of Allah, is better and more lasting; therefore, you ought to prefer it over the enjoyment and adornment of the world. Do you not then exercise your intellect?

The verse is a third response to their saying: **إِنْ نَتَّبِعِ الْهُدى مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا**. Its gist is: Let us grant that if you follow the guidance, the Arabs will snatch you from your land; however, what you would lose is merely the enjoyment and fleeting adornment of the life of this world. Why then do you prefer it over the reward with Allah for following the guidance and the felicity of the life of the Hereafter, which is better and more lasting?

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَفَمَنْ وَعَدْناهُ وَعْداً حَسَناً فَهُوَ لاقِيهِ كَمَنْ مَتَّعْناهُ مَتاعَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا ثُمَّ هُوَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ مِنَ الْمُحْضَرِينَ** — **Is he whom We have promised a goodly promise, which he will receive, like him whom We have provided with the wares of the life of this world, but who will be among those summoned on the Day of Resurrection?** This verse, along with the following seven verses, clarifies the content of the previous verse—which is that preferring to follow the guidance is better than abandoning it to enjoy the wares of the life of this world—through another explanation. This explanation compares the state of one who follows the guidance and receives the goodly promise Allah has made, with the state of one who does not follow it, sufficing himself with the enjoyment of the world, only to face being "summoned" on the Day of Resurrection, where his deities will disown him, fail to answer his call, and he will witness the punishment and be questioned about his response to the messengers.

Thus, His saying: **أَفَمَنْ وَعَدْناهُ وَعْداً حَسَناً فَهُوَ لاقِيهِ** (Is he whom We have promised a goodly promise, which he will receive...) is a rhetorical question of denial (استفهام إنكاري). The "goodly promise" is His promise of forgiveness and Paradise, as He said: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ** — **Allah has promised those who have faith and do righteous deeds that they shall have forgiveness and a great reward** (5:9). Allah's promise is never false; He said: **أَلا إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهُ حَقٌّ** — **Look! Allah’s promise is indeed true** (10:55).

His saying: **كَمَنْ مَتَّعْناهُ مَتاعَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** (...like him whom We have provided with the wares of the life of this world) means one who is deprived of that goodly promise because he restricted himself to the enjoyment of its wares. The evidence for this restriction is the contrast between the "promise" and the "provision of enjoyment."

His saying: **ثُمَّ هُوَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ مِنَ الْمُحْضَرِينَ** (...but who will be among those summoned on the Day of Resurrection) means summoned for punishment, or for questioning and accountability. The word **then** (ثُمَ) indicates the sequence of the discourse. The use of a nominal sentence (جملة اسمية), as in the corresponding phrase **فَهُوَ لاقِيهِ** (which he will receive), indicates certainty and realization (التحقق).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَيَوْمَ يُنادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكائِيَ الَّذِينَ كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ** — **The day He will call them and say, ‘Where are My partners whom you used to claim?’** The "partners" are those they used to worship in the world. They were considered partners by them because they accorded them, or attributed to them, some of what belongs to Allah's affairs, such as worship and governance (التدبير). In His saying **يُنادِيهِمْ** (He will call them), there is an allusion to their distance and abandonment on that day.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قالَ الَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ الَّذِينَ أَغْوَيْنا أَغْوَيْناهُمْ كَما غَوَيْنا** — **Those against whom the word has become due will say, ‘Our Lord! These are the ones we perverted. We perverted them as we were perverted ourselves.’** Their deities, whom they regarded as partners to Allah, the Glorified, are of two types. One type consists of honored servants of Allah, such as the near-stationed angels and Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him). The other type consists of the rebellious jinn and those among humans who claimed divinity, like Pharaoh, Nimrod, and others. Allah has also grouped with them everyone who is followed in falsehood, such as Iblis, the demonic companions (قرناء الشياطين), and the leaders of misguidance (أئمة الضلال), as He said: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ** — **Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, “Do not worship Satan...** until He said: **وَلَقَدْ أَضَلَّ مِنْكُمْ جِبِلًّا كَثِيراً** — **He has certainly led astray many a generation of you** (36:60-62). And He said: **أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ** — **Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god?** (45:23). And He said: **اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَرُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** — **They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah** (9:31).

Those referred to by His saying **قالَ الَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ** (Those against whom the word has become due will say) are of the second type, as evidenced by their mention of perverting others and their disowning of their worship. Although these polytheists themselves are also among those against whom the word has become due—as indicated by His saying: **حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ مِنِّي لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ** — **the word from Me has come true: ‘I will surely fill hell with jinn and humans, all together’** (32:13)—the ones intended in the verse under discussion are the followed ones (المتبوعون) from whom polytheism and misguidance originated.

The mention of the words of these "partners," without mentioning that the questioned ones pointed to them, is perhaps to indicate that they were lost to them in this station, as in His saying: **وَيَوْمَ يُنادِيهِمْ أَيْنَ شُرَكائِي قالُوا آذَنَّاكَ ما مِنَّا مِنْ شَهِيدٍ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَدْعُونَ مِنْ قَبْلُ** — **The day He will call them, ‘Where are My partners?’ they will say, ‘We inform You that there is no witness among us.’ And what they used to invoke before will forsake them** (41:47-48).

His saying: **رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ الَّذِينَ أَغْوَيْنا** (Our Lord! These are the ones we perverted) — they point to the polytheists — "these are the ones we perverted." This sentence is a preamble to the following one.

His saying: **أَغْوَيْناهُمْ كَما غَوَيْنا** (We perverted them as we were perverted ourselves) means: their perversion was through our perverting them because of our own perversion. Just as we were perverted by our own choice without compulsion (إلجاء), they too were perverted by their own choice without compulsion. The evidence for this meaning is what Allah narrated from Iblis on that day when he said: **وَما كانَ لِي عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا أَنْ دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَاسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِي فَلا تَلُومُونِي وَلُومُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ** — **I had no authority over you, except that I called you and you responded to me. So do not blame me, but blame yourselves** (14:22). And He said, narrating the mutual questioning of the wrongdoers and their companions: **وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ قالُوا إِنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَأْتُونَنا عَنِ الْيَمِينِ قالُوا بَلْ لَمْ تَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ وَما كانَ لَنا عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ بَلْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً طاغِينَ فَحَقَّ عَلَيْنا قَوْلُ رَبِّنا إِنَّا لَذائِقُونَ فَأَغْوَيْناكُمْ إِنَّا كُنَّا غاوِينَ** — **They will turn to one another, questioning each other. They will say, ‘Indeed you used to come to us from the right.’ They will say, ‘No, you [yourselves] were not faithful. We had no authority over you. Rather you were a rebellious people. So our Lord’s word has become due against us: we shall indeed taste [the punishment]. So we perverted you, for we were perverted ourselves’** (37:27-32). That is: nothing could reach you from us, while we were perverted, except perversion.

From here, it appears that their saying **أَغْوَيْناهُمْ كَما غَوَيْنا** has another meaning: that they acquired a description similar to what was in us, except that we disown them since we did not compel them to perversion; they did not worship us by compulsion.

His saying: **تَبَرَّأْنا إِلَيْكَ** (We disown them before You) is an absolute disavowal of them, since they had no right to compel them or strip them of their choice. His saying: **ما كانُوا إِيَّانا يَعْبُدُونَ** (It was not us that they used to worship) means: not by our compulsion, or it refers to our disowning of their deeds, for whoever disowns a deed is not associated with it. To this meaning return the sayings of Allah in various places of His speech describing this station: **وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ** — **and what they used to fabricate will forsake them** (6:24); **وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَدْعُونَ مِنْ قَبْلُ** — **And what they used to invoke before will forsake them** (41:48); **وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا مَكانَكُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَشُرَكاؤُكُمْ فَزَيَّلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ وَقالَ شُرَكاؤُهُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ إِيَّانا يَعْبُدُونَ** — **On the day We will muster them all together, then We will say to the polytheists, ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’ Then We will set them apart from one another, and their partners will say, ‘It was not us that you used to worship’** (10:28), and other such verses. Reflect on this.

It has been said: the meaning is "We disown their deeds before You; it was not us they used to worship, but rather they used to worship their own desires or they used to worship the devils." This is not free from weakness.

Because each of their sayings **تَبَرَّأْنا إِلَيْكَ** and **ما كانُوا إِيَّانا يَعْبُدُونَ** carries the meaning of their saying **أَغْوَيْناهُمْ كَما غَوَيْنا**, they were presented as separate clauses without a coordinating conjunction (عطف).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَقِيلَ ادْعُوا شُرَكاءَكُمْ فَدَعَوْهُمْ فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَهُمْ وَرَأَوُا الْعَذابَ لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ كانُوا يَهْتَدُونَ** — **It will be said, ‘Invoke your partners!’ So they will invoke them, but they will not respond to them, and they will see the punishment. If only they had been guided!** The "partners" refer to the deities who were partners to Allah in their claim; hence He attributed them to them. The "invoking" means calling upon them to help them and ward off the punishment; this is why He said **وَرَأَوُا الْعَذابَ** (and they will see the punishment) after saying **فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَهُمْ** (but they will not respond to them).

Regarding His saying: **لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ كانُوا يَهْتَدُونَ** (If only they had been guided!), it is said that the apodosis (جواب) of *law* is suppressed because the context indicates it, the estimation being: "If they had been guided, they would have seen the punishment," meaning they would have believed that the punishment is true. It is also possible that *law* is for wishing (تمني), meaning: "Would that they had been guided!"

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَيَوْمَ يُنادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ ما ذا أَجَبْتُمُ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** — **The day He will call them and say, ‘What was the answer you gave to the messengers?’** This is coordinated with His previous saying **وَيَوْمَ يُنادِيهِمْ** etc. They are questioned first about their partners and ordered to seek their help, and secondly about their response to the messengers sent to them from Allah.

The meaning is: What did you say in response to those whom Allah sent to you as messengers, calling you to faith and righteous deeds?

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَعَمِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَنْباءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَهُمْ لا يَتَساءَلُونَ** — **On that day all news will be obscured for them, and they will not [even] ask one another.** "Blindness" (العمى) is a metaphor (استعارة) for making a person such that they cannot find their way to any news. The apparent requirement was to attribute blindness to them, not to the news, but the matter was reversed, and it was said: **فَعَمِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَنْباءُ** (the news will be obscured/blinded for them) to indicate their being seized from every side, the blocking of all paths, and the severing of all means, as He said: **وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبابُ** — **and all their means are cut off** (2:166). Due to the failure of means to have any effect on that day, news does not find its way to them, and they find nothing with which to excuse themselves to escape the punishment.

His saying: **فَهُمْ لا يَتَساءَلُونَ** (and they will not ask one another) is a derivation (تفريع) from the obscuring of news, like the derivation of some particulars of a general rule from it. That is, they will not ask one another to prepare an excuse for their denial of the messengers and their rejection of the call.

The beginning and end of the verse have been interpreted with many different explanations that are of no benefit to mention, so we deemed it better to overlook them.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَأَمَّا مَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً فَعَسى أَنْ يَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُفْلِحِينَ** — **As for him who repents and believes and acts righteously, maybe he will be among the felicitous.** That is: this is the state of one who disbelieved and did not return to Allah, the Glorified; but as for him who returns, believes, and acts righteously, it is hoped that he will be among the felicitous. **Maybe** (عَسى)—as has been said—indicates certainty (التحقيق) according to the custom of the Generous, or it indicates hope (الترجي) on the part of the repentant one. The meaning is: let him expect success.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَرَبُّكَ يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ وَيَخْتارُ ما كانَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ سُبْحانَ اللهِ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** — **Your Lord creates whatever He wishes and chooses. They have no choice. Immaculate is Allah and exalted is He above the partners they ascribe [to Him]!** **Choice** (الخيرة) is in the sense of choosing (التخير), like *al-tiyarah* is in the sense of *al-tatayyur* (taking omens).

The verse is a fourth response to their saying: **إِنْ نَتَّبِعِ الْهُدى مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا**, and it contains a decisive proof.

The explanation of this is: Creation (الخلق), which is making and bringing into existence, terminates in Him, the Exalted, as He said: **اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — **Allah is the creator of all things** (39:62). Thus, there is no agent in existence with the true meaning of agency other than Him, the Exalted. There is nothing that can compel Him, the Exalted, to any action, for such a supposed thing would either be created by Him, its existence terminating in Him—in which case its existence and the effects of its existence terminate in Him, and it is meaningless for a thing or its effect to influence itself—or it would be uncreated by Him and not terminating in Him, influencing Him through compulsion and coercion. But there is no agent in existence other than Him, nor is there anything whose existence does not terminate in Him. Thus, nothing gives Him an effect, nor does anything prevent Him from an effect, as He said: **وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ** — **Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment** (13:41), and He said: **وَاللهُ غالِبٌ عَلى أَمْرِهِ** — **Allah has full command over His affairs** (12:21).

Since there is no overcomer to coerce Him into an action, nor any preventer to stop Him from an action, He is "choosing" (مختار) in the true sense of choice. This is according to the ontological realm (التكوين), and the legislative realm (التشريع) follows it. For the reality of legislation is that He fashioned humans upon a primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — which does not remain upright except by performing certain matters, which are the obligations and what is in their ruling, and abandoning certain matters, which are the prohibitions and what is in their ruling. Thus, what benefits a human in their perfection and felicity is what He commanded and exhorted toward, and what harms them is what He forbade and warned against.

Therefore, He, the Exalted, has the right to choose in the stage of legislation whatever rulings and laws He wishes, just as He has the right to choose in the stage of creation whatever making and governance He wishes. This is the meaning of His saying: **وَرَبُّكَ يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ وَيَخْتارُ** (Your Lord creates whatever He wishes and chooses), and it is stated absolutely.

It appears that His saying **يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ** (creates whatever He wishes) refers to His ontological choice (اختياره التكويني), for its absolute sense means that His power is not deficient in creating anything, nor does anything prevent Him from what He wishes. In other words, nothing is impossible for His will, neither by itself nor by a preventer; this is choice in its true sense. His saying **وَيَخْتارُ** (and chooses) refers to His legislative and nominal choice (اختياره التشريعي الاعتباري). Its coordination with **يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ** is the coordination of the effect with its cause, because legislation and nominality are derived from creation and reality.

It is possible to interpret **يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ** as ontological choice and **وَيَخْتارُ** as encompassing both reality and nominality, but the previous view is more appropriate. Evidence for it is that what is negated in the subsequent saying **ما كانَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ** (They have no choice) is legislative nominal choice, and the choice affirmed in His saying **وَيَخْتارُ** corresponds to it; thus, the intent is to affirm legislative nominal choice.

Furthermore, there is no doubt that a human has ontological choice regarding the actions originating from them through knowledge and will, even if it is not an absolute choice. External causes and conditions have a role in their actions; for example, their eating a morsel of food depends on the external existence of the food, its suitability, its proximity, and the assistance of the tools for taking, grasping, biting, chewing, swallowing, and other countless factors. Thus, the issuance of a voluntary action from them is conditional upon the agreement of external and internal causes in the realization of their action. Allah, the Glorified, is at the head of all those causes, and everything terminates in Him. He is the One who created the human characterized by the attribute of choice and gave them their choice just as He gave them their creation.

Then, the human naturally sees for themselves a legislative nominal choice in what they wish to do or leave, corresponding to their ontological choice. They have the right to do what they wish and leave what they wish without any of their fellow humans having the right to compel them to something or prevent them from something, because they are like them, not exceeding them in the meaning of humanity, nor possessing anything of them. This is what is meant by the human being naturally free.

Thus, the human is choosing in themselves and naturally free, unless someone else possesses something of them, thereby stripping themselves of freedom. This is like the social human who strips themselves of freedom regarding the instances of established customs and laws in their society by entering into the society and endorsing the customs and laws that run within it, whether they be religious or social. It is also like two combatants, each of whom possesses of the other what he overcomes; the victor has the right to do with his prisoner what he wishes. It is also like the employee who sells his labor and hires himself out; he is not free in his work, for being owned is incompatible with freedom.

So, the human, in relation to the rest of their kind, is free in their work and choosing in their action, unless they strip away some of their choice by their own choice, thereby giving ownership to another. But Allah, the Glorified, owns the human in their self and in their action originating from them with an absolute ownership—through ontological ownership and through conventional nominal ownership. Thus, they have no choice (خيرة) and no freedom regarding what He wants from them legislatively through a legislative command or prohibition, just as they have no choice and no freedom regarding what He wishes through His ontological will.

This is what is intended by His saying: **ما كانَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ** (They have no choice). That is, they have no choice if Allah, the Glorified, chooses for them something to do or leave, such that they could choose for themselves what they wish even if it opposes what Allah has chosen. The verse is close in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: **وَما كانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ وَلا مُؤْمِنَةٍ إِذا قَضَى اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ أَمْراً أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ مِنْ أَمْرِهِمْ** — **A faithful man or woman may not have any option in their matter, when Allah and His Apostle have decided on a matter** (33:36). The commentators have various unhelpful opinions regarding the interpretation of the verse, which we have overlooked; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the lengthy works.

His saying: **سُبْحانَ اللهِ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** — **Immaculate is Allah and exalted is He above the partners they ascribe [to Him]!** That is, above their polytheism in choosing idols as deities to worship besides Allah.

There is another, more subtle meaning: He is sanctified and exalted above their polytheism in claiming that they have a choice regarding what He, the Exalted, chooses, by accepting or rejecting it. For choice in this sense is not complete except with the claim of independence in existence (الاستقلال في الوجود) and self-sufficiency from Him, the Exalted, and it is not complete except with sharing with Him, the Exalted, in the attribute of divinity (الألوهية).

In His saying **وَرَبُّكَ يَخْلُقُ** (And your Lord creates), there is a shift from the first-person plural to the third-person singular. The subtle point in this is to support the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), strengthen him, and comfort his soul by adding the attribute of "Lord" (الرب) to him. For its meaning is that the ruling He sent him with is effective and not to be rejected; they have no choice in accepting or rejecting it, and because they do not accept His Lordship.

In His saying **سُبْحانَ اللهِ** (Immaculate is Allah), the manifest noun is placed where a pronoun would be. The subtle point in this is to return the matter to the Transcendent Essence (الذات المتعالية) which is the source of sanctification and exaltation above everything that does not befit the courtyard of His holiness. For He, the Exalted, is characterized by every perfection and sanctified from every deficiency, because He is Allah, exalted is His Name.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَرَبُّكَ يَعْلَمُ ما تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَما يُعْلِنُونَ** — **Your Lord knows what their breasts conceal and what they disclose.** **Concealing** (الإكنان) is hiding, and **disclosing** (الإعلان) is manifesting. Because the breast is considered a storehouse for secrets, concealing was attributed to the breasts, while disclosing was attributed to themselves.

Perhaps following the previous verse with this one is to indicate that He, the Exalted, only chose for them what He chose because of His knowledge of the filth of polytheism and sin in their outward and inward states; thus, He purified them with that through His wisdom.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَهُوَ اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْأُولى وَالْآخِرَةِ وَلَهُ الْحُكْمُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ** — **He is Allah, there is no god except Him. To Him belongs all praise in this world and the Hereafter, and His is the judgement, and to Him you will be brought back.** The apparent context is that the pronoun at the beginning of the verse refers to **your Lord** (رَبُّكَ) in the previous verse. Based on this, it appears that the *lam* in the Name of Majesty (اسم الجلالة) is to allude to the meaning of the attribute. His saying **لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** (there is no god except Him) is an emphasis of the exclusivity (الحصر) understood from His saying **هُوَ اللهُ** (He is Allah), as if it were said: "He is the Deity—characterized alone with divinity—there is no god except Him."

Accordingly, the verse is like a completion of the explanation of the previous verse, as if it were said: He, the Glorified, is "choosing"; He has the right to choose for them that they worship Him alone. He knows their outward and inward, so He has the right to decree for them that they worship Him alone. He is the Deity worthy of worship alone, so it is obligatory for them to worship Him alone.

The phrases at the end of the verse, from His saying **لَهُ الْحَمْدُ** (To Him belongs all praise) etc., are three reasons justifying His being the only Deity worthy of worship.

As for His saying **لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْأُولى وَالْآخِرَةِ** (To Him belongs all praise in this world and the Hereafter), it is because every perfection existing in the world and the Hereafter is a blessing descending from Him, the Exalted, for which He deserves beautiful praise. Every beauty among these bestowed blessings emanates from an essential perfection among His essential attributes, for which He deserves praise. Thus, all praise belongs to Him, and nothing else is independent in any praise it is praised with except that it terminates in Him. Worship is praise through word or deed; therefore, He is the only Deity worthy of worship.

As for His saying **وَلَهُ الْحُكْمُ** (and His is the judgement), it is because He, the Glorified, is the absolute Owner. No one else owns anything except what He has given them ownership of, and He is the Owner of what He has given them. He, the Glorified, is the Owner in the stage of legislation and nominality, just as He is the Owner in the stage of creation and reality. Among the effects of His ownership is that He decrees for His servants and those He owns that they worship none but Him.

As for His saying **وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ** (and to Him you will be brought back), it is because the return is for accounting and recompense. Since He is the destination of return, He is the One who accounts and recompenses. Since He alone is the One who accounts and recompenses, He is the One who must be worshipped alone, and He has a religion through which He alone must be worshipped.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ جَعَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكُمُ اللَّيْلَ سَرْمَداً إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ** — **Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah were to make the night perpetual for you until the Day of Resurrection...** until the end of the verse. **Perpetual** (السرمد) is on the pattern of *fa‘lal*, meaning permanent. It is said to be from *al-sard* (succession) with an added *mim*, meaning continuous and regular. Its restriction to the Day of Resurrection is because there is no night after the Day of Resurrection.

His saying: **مَنْ إِلهٌ غَيْرُ اللهِ يَأْتِيكُمْ بِضِياءٍ** — **...who is the god besides Allah who may bring you light?** That is: who is the deity who can revoke the decree of Allah, the Exalted, and bring you light by which you may see and strive in seeking livelihood? This is what the context testifies to, and its parallel occurs in His subsequent saying: **مَنْ إِلهٌ غَيْرُ اللهِ يَأْتِيكُمْ بِلَيْلٍ** etc.

By this, the objection raised against the two verses is refuted: that if the making of the night perpetual until the Day of Resurrection were assumed to be realized, the bringing of light would not be conceivable at all. For the one who brings it would either be Allah, the Exalted, or someone else. As for someone else, their inability is manifest. As for Allah, the Exalted, His bringing it would necessitate the meeting of night and day, which is impossible, and power and will do not pertain to the impossible. The same applies to the side of the day.

It has sometimes been answered that the meaning of His saying **إِنْ جَعَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكُمُ** (if Allah were to make for you) is "if Allah *willed* to make for you." This is as you see (i.e., weak).

The apparent requirement was to say: "who is the god besides Allah who may bring you *day*," as required by the context of the contrast between night and day in the speech. However, the shift to mentioning "light" (ضياء) instead of "day" is a case of compelling the argument with the easiest and simplest thing that can be supposed, so that the falsehood of the opponent's claim appears most clearly. It is as if it were said: "If there were a deity other than Him, the Exalted, governing the affairs of the world, then if Allah made the night perpetual, let him be able to bring the day; we go further: let him be able to bring some light by which you may see." But nothing has power over that; all power belongs to Allah, the Glorified.

A similar reasoning does not apply to the following verse regarding the night, such that it could be said, for example: "who is the god besides Allah who may bring you *darkness*." For if what is brought were some darkness, it would not suffice for rest, and if it were extended darkness, it would be the night itself.

The use of the indefinite **light** (بِضِياءٍ) supports the reason mentioned. Other reasons have been cited for this, which are not free from forced interpretation (تعسف).

His saying: **أَفَلا تَسْمَعُونَ** — **Will you not then listen?** That is, a listening of understanding and reflection, so that you may reflect and understand that there is no god other than Him, the Exalted.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ جَعَلَ اللهُ عَلَيْكُمُ النَّهارَ سَرْمَداً إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ مَنْ إِلهٌ غَيْرُ اللهِ يَأْتِيكُمْ بِلَيْلٍ تَسْكُنُونَ فِيهِ** — **Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah were to make the day perpetual for you until the Day of Resurrection, who is the god besides Allah who may bring you night wherein you may rest?’** That is, rest therein from the fatigue of striving for livelihood that has afflicted you.

His saying: **أَفَلا تُبْصِرُونَ** — **Will you not then see?** That is, a seeing of understanding and remembrance. Since they did not see and did not listen, they are blind and deaf. It is elegant that the two verses conclude with **أَفَلا تَسْمَعُونَ** (Will you not then listen?) and **أَفَلا تُبْصِرُونَ** (Will you not then see?). Perhaps the verse of the day was specifically assigned "seeing" because of the suitability of the day's light for seeing, leaving "listening" for the verse of the night, which is not without suitability for it.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَمِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ لِتَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** — **Out of His mercy He has made for you the night and the day, that you may rest therein and that you may seek His grace, and so that you may give thanks.** The verse is like the conclusion of the argument mentioned in the two previous verses, presented after the refutation of the opponent's claim in the form of an initial report, due to its establishment without any challenger.

His saying: **لِتَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ** (that you may rest therein) — the *lam* is for causality and the pronoun refers to the night. That is: He made the night for you so that you may rest therein. His saying: **لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ** (and that you may seek His grace) means: and He made the day for you so that you may seek His provision, which is His gift. Thus, the return of "that you may rest" and "that you may seek" to the night and the day is by way of ordered distribution (اللف والنشر المرتب). His saying: **وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** (and so that you may give thanks) refers to both of them together.

His saying: **وَمِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ جَعَلَ لَكُمُ** (Out of His mercy He has made for you) is in the sense of our saying: "He made for you, and that is a mercy from Him." In it is an allusion that creation—such as rest and seeking—and legislation—which is their guidance toward gratitude—are among the effects of His attribute of mercy. Understand this.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَيَوْمَ يُنادِيهِمْ فَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكائِيَ الَّذِينَ كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ** — **The day He will call them and say, ‘Where are My partners whom you used to claim?’** Its interpretation has preceded, and the verse is repeated because the content of the following verse requires it.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَنَزَعْنا مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيداً فَقُلْنا هاتُوا بُرْهانَكُمْ** — **We shall draw a witness from every nation and say, ‘Produce your evidence!’** until the end of the verse. This is an allusion to the manifestation of the falsehood of their claim to them on the Day of Resurrection. The "witness" refers to the witness of deeds (شهيد الأعمال)—as has been mentioned repeatedly—and the verse does not explicitly indicate that he is the prophet sent to the nation, given the singular "witness" and the mention of "nation" (أمة), for a nation is a group of people and does not explicitly or textually refer to the group to whom a prophet was sent, even if they are among its instances.

His saying: **فَقُلْنا هاتُوا بُرْهانَكُمْ** (and say, ‘Produce your evidence!’) means: We demanded from them the decisive proof for their claim that Allah has partners.

His saying: **فَعَلِمُوا أَنَّ الْحَقَّ لِلَّهِ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ** — **Then they will know that the truth belongs to Allah, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them.** That is: their false claim that Allah, the Glorified, has partners will disappear from them; at that point, they will know that the truth regarding divinity belongs to Allah alone. Thus, "forsaking" (الضلال) means disappearance by way of metaphor. This is how they interpreted it; thus, there is a transposition (تقديم وتأخير) in the speech, the original being: "and what they used to fabricate will forsake them, then they will know that the truth belongs to Allah."

Based on this, His saying **أَنَّ الْحَقَّ لِلَّهِ** (that the truth belongs to Allah) is like what is said in a judgment between two litigants when they both claim a right for themselves: "the right belongs to so-and-so, not so-and-so." It is as if He, the Exalted, is litigating with the polytheists when they claim that divinity—in the sense of being worthy of worship—is a right for their partners, while He, the Exalted, claims it is His right. He demands their evidence for their claim, and the evidence fails them; at that point, they know that this right belongs to Allah. For divinity is a established truth about which there is no doubt; if it is not a right for anyone other than Him, the Exalted, then it is a right for Him.

This is a view that is outwardly plausible and acceptable. However, the reality provided by His speech, the Exalted, is that among the characteristics of the Day of Resurrection is that the truth (الحق) becomes pure therein through a witnessed manifestation with no veil over it. Through it, every falsehood that obscures the matter and resembles the truth is removed. Its necessary consequence is that the matter of divinity appears with a manifestation that has no veil, and through it, the fabrication of partners is removed in a manner resulting from it—not that the evidence for the partners is found missing, from which His oneness in divinity is concluded by way of intellectual argumentation. Understand this.

By this, first, the objection to the previous view is refuted: that what is understood from His speech, the Exalted, is that they have no rational proof for their claim, and thus there is no reason for their knowledge that the truth belongs to Allah to be delayed until the Day of Resurrection. Second, the talk of transposition mentioned above—which has no apparent subtle point except for the sake of rhyme—is removed.

It is possible that **the truth** (الْحَقَ) in His saying **فَعَلِمُوا أَنَّ الْحَقَّ لِلَّهِ** is a verbal noun (مصدر), so the meaning of the sentence returns to the meaning of His saying: **وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ الْمُبِينُ** — **and they will know that Allah is the Manifest Truth** (24:25). Thus, the truth being for Allah means His being, the Exalted, the Truth—if the Truth in His essence is intended—or its terminating in Him and subsisting through Him—if something other than Him is intended—as He, the Exalted, said: **الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ** — **The truth is from your Lord** (3:60), and He did not say: "The truth is with your Lord."

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالُوا إِنْ نَتَّبِعِ الْهُدى مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا**, it is said: "It was revealed concerning Quraysh—when the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called them to Islam and migration—and they said: 'If we follow the guidance with you, we will be snatched away from our land.' So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **أَوَلَمْ نُمَكِّنْ لَهُمْ حَرَماً آمِناً ـ يُجْبى إِلَيْهِ ثَمَراتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ رِزْقاً مِنْ لَدُنَّا ـ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ**."

**I say**: This meaning was also narrated in *Kashf al-Mahajjah* and *Rawdat al-Wa‘izin* by al-Mufid. It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh, from Ibn Abbas.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Nasa'i and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Abbas: "It was al-Harith ibn ‘Amir ibn Nawfal who said: **إِنْ نَتَّبِعِ الْهُدى مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا**."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَرَبُّكَ يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ وَيَخْتارُ ما كانَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ**, it is said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, chooses the Imam, and they have no right to choose."

**I say**: This is a case of application (الجري) based on the necessity of the appointment of the infallible Imam by Allah, the Exalted, just like the Prophet. Detailed discussion on this has preceded.

In the same work, in a narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَزَعْنا مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيداً**, he said: "From this nation, its Imam."

**I say**: This is also a case of application (الجري).
